content.text: Integration vs E2E Tests for AI Workflows

Integration tests and end-to-end tests both involve real LLM calls, but they answer different questions. An integration test asks: do the components of one AI pipeline compose correctly under controlled conditions? An end-to-end test asks: does the complete user-facing journey work, from browser click to rendered AI response? The distinction governs what you control, what you assert, how you handle non-determinism, and how often you run each.

Conflating the two is expensive: integration tests written at the E2E scope are slow, flaky, and hard to debug. E2E tests written at the integration scope miss the browser, routing, and streaming layers that users actually touch.

attrs.alt: Integration tests verify one AI pipeline with controlled data; E2E tests verify the full user journey through browser and API

content.text: How Integration Tests Differ from E2E Tests
Dimension
Integration Test
E2E Test
Scope
One AI pipeline (retrieval → LLM → parser)
Full user journey (browser → API → LLM → render)
Environment
Test environment, controlled corpus
Staging or production-like, real data
LLM
Real, but same prompt every time
Real, user-driven prompt — higher variance
Assertions
Schema, latency, citation presence, parse success
UI element visible, no error, keyword present
Non-determinism
Mitigated by fixed corpus + presence checks
Accepted — assert structure, not exact text
Speed
~30s per test
~60–120s per test
When to run
On merge to main
Post-deploy to staging / pre-release
Integration Test: Verifying One AI Pipeline

An integration test exercises the real retrieval, real prompt builder, and real LLM, but uses a committed test corpus so the retrieved chunks are the same every run. Assertions target the structure of the output — not its exact wording — plus measurable properties like latency and citation presence.

# tests/integration/test_rag_pipeline.py
"""
Integration test for the RAG summarise pipeline.
Requires: INTEGRATION_TEST_ENV=true, OPENAI_API_KEY set.
Uses: tests/fixtures/corpus/ — 10 committed markdown documents.
"""
import pytest, asyncio, time
from pathlib import Path
from agents.rag_pipeline import RAGPipeline, RAGResult
from retrieval.in_memory_index import InMemoryIndex

FIXTURE_CORPUS = list(Path("tests/fixtures/corpus").glob("*.md"))

@pytest.fixture(scope="module")
def pipeline():
    index = InMemoryIndex.from_files(FIXTURE_CORPUS)
    return RAGPipeline(index=index)


@pytest.mark.skipif(not __import__("os").getenv("INTEGRATION_TEST_ENV"),
                    reason="set INTEGRATION_TEST_ENV=true")
class TestRAGPipelineIntegration:

    @pytest.mark.asyncio
    async def test_result_has_required_fields(self, pipeline):
        result: RAGResult = await pipeline.run(query="What is RAG?")
        assert result.summary, "summary must be non-empty"
        assert isinstance(result.citations, list), "citations must be a list"
        assert result.latency_ms > 0

    @pytest.mark.asyncio
    async def test_citations_reference_corpus_documents(self, pipeline):
        result = await pipeline.run(query="What is RAG?")
        corpus_ids = {p.stem for p in FIXTURE_CORPUS}
        for cite in result.citations:
            assert cite.doc_id in corpus_ids, (
                f"Citation '{cite.doc_id}' not in test corpus"
            )

    @pytest.mark.asyncio
    async def test_latency_within_slo(self, pipeline):
        result = await pipeline.run(query="Short factual question.")
        assert result.latency_ms < 10_000, f"Too slow: {result.latency_ms}ms"

    @pytest.mark.asyncio
    async def test_empty_corpus_returns_graceful_response(self):
        empty_index = InMemoryIndex.from_files([])
        p = RAGPipeline(index=empty_index)
        result = await p.run(query="What is RAG?")
        assert result.summary  # must not raise
        assert result.citations == []

    @pytest.mark.asyncio
    async def test_concurrent_requests_do_not_degrade(self, pipeline):
        queries = ["What is RAG?", "Explain RLHF.", "What is a vector database?"]
        start = time.monotonic()
        results = await asyncio.gather(*[pipeline.run(q) for q in queries])
        elapsed = time.monotonic() - start
        assert all(r.summary for r in results)
        # Three concurrent requests should complete faster than three serial ones
        assert elapsed < 25.0, f"Concurrent requests too slow: {elapsed:.1f}s"
Controlling Non-Determinism in Integration Tests
Real LLM calls produce different text on every run. Integration tests must be written to pass regardless of the exact wording. Three techniques make integration tests reliably green:
Fixed corpus:
the retrieved chunks are the same every run because the index is built from committed test files, not a live database. This eliminates retrieval variance.
Structural assertions:
assert that the response JSON parses, that required fields are non-null, and that citations reference documents that exist — not that the summary text matches a golden string.
Temperature 0:
for integration tests, set temperature=0 in the LLM call. Most models are more (but not perfectly) deterministic at zero temperature. Combine with structural assertions for reliability.
E2E Tests: Verifying the User-Facing Journey

E2E tests use Playwright to drive a real browser against a staging environment. They verify that the AI response appears in the UI without an error state and that streaming renders correctly. They do not assert exact text — LLM output varies too much for that.

# tests/e2e/test_summarise_flow.py
"""
E2E tests for the AI summarise feature.
Requires: E2E_BASE_URL and E2E_TEST_USER_TOKEN set in env.
Run: pytest tests/e2e/ --timeout=120 (post-deploy to staging)
"""
import os, pytest
from playwright.sync_api import Page, expect

BASE_URL = os.environ.get("E2E_BASE_URL", "https://staging.example.com")

@pytest.fixture(scope="session")
def authenticated_page(browser):
    context = browser.new_context(
        storage_state=None,
        extra_http_headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {os.environ['E2E_TEST_USER_TOKEN']}"},
    )
    page = context.new_page()
    yield page
    context.close()


class TestSummariseFlow:
    def test_submit_query_renders_response(self, authenticated_page: Page):
        page = authenticated_page
        page.goto(f"{BASE_URL}/summarise")

        # Submit a query
        page.fill("[data-testid='query-input']", "What is retrieval-augmented generation?")
        page.click("[data-testid='submit-btn']")

        # Response area should appear and not show an error
        response_el = page.locator("[data-testid='ai-response']")
        expect(response_el).to_be_visible(timeout=30_000)

        # Do NOT assert exact text — assert presence of meaningful content
        response_text = response_el.inner_text()
        assert len(response_text) > 20, "Response too short"
        assert "error" not in response_text.lower(), "Response contains error text"

    def test_streaming_indicator_disappears(self, authenticated_page: Page):
        page = authenticated_page
        page.goto(f"{BASE_URL}/summarise")
        page.fill("[data-testid='query-input']", "Short question.")
        page.click("[data-testid='submit-btn']")

        # Loading spinner must appear then disappear
        expect(page.locator("[data-testid='loading-spinner']")).to_be_visible(timeout=5_000)
        expect(page.locator("[data-testid='loading-spinner']")).to_be_hidden(timeout=60_000)

    def test_empty_query_shows_validation_error(self, authenticated_page: Page):
        page = authenticated_page
        page.goto(f"{BASE_URL}/summarise")
        page.click("[data-testid='submit-btn']")  # submit with empty input
        expect(page.locator("[data-testid='validation-error']")).to_be_visible(timeout=2_000)

    def test_network_error_shows_friendly_message(self, authenticated_page: Page):
        page = authenticated_page
        # Intercept the API call and simulate failure
        page.route("**/v1/summarise", lambda route: route.abort("failed"))
        page.goto(f"{BASE_URL}/summarise")
        page.fill("[data-testid='query-input']", "Any question.")
        page.click("[data-testid='submit-btn']")
        expect(page.locator("[data-testid='error-banner']")).to_be_visible(timeout=10_000)
        page.unroute("**/v1/summarise")
When to Write an Integration Test vs an E2E Test
Use the scope of the failure to guide the choice. Integration tests own the AI pipeline boundary; E2E tests own the user-experience boundary.
Failure you want to catch
Write
Retriever returns wrong schema after a library upgrade
Integration
Prompt template produces malformed JSON that the parser rejects
Integration
LLM latency exceeds 10s for a known-size corpus
Integration
Citations list is empty even when documents are retrieved
Integration
Submit button triggers the request but response never renders
E2E
Streaming chunks appear out of order in the UI
E2E
Auth token is not forwarded from browser to API gateway
E2E
Error from the LLM shows a stack trace instead of a friendly message
E2E
Managing Test Fixtures and Pre-Computed Embeddings

Integration tests that call the embedding API on every run are slow and add unnecessary cost. Commit pre-computed embeddings as sidecar files alongside the test corpus. A setup script computes them once; CI reads them from disk. If the embedding model changes, re-run the script and commit the new sidecars — that version-controls the embedding change explicitly.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# scripts/precompute_test_embeddings.py
"""
Run once whenever tests/fixtures/corpus/ changes or the embedding model changes.
Writes .embed.json sidecar files next to each .md file.
Commit the sidecar files to make integration tests fast and free.
"""
import json, os
from pathlib import Path
import openai

CORPUS_DIR = Path("tests/fixtures/corpus")
MODEL = "text-embedding-3-small"
BATCH_SIZE = 20

def embed_texts(texts: list[str]) -> list[list[float]]:
    client = openai.OpenAI()
    response = client.embeddings.create(input=texts, model=MODEL)
    return [d.embedding for d in response.data]

def main():
    md_files = sorted(CORPUS_DIR.glob("*.md"))
    sidecar_missing = [f for f in md_files if not f.with_suffix(".embed.json").exists()]
    if not sidecar_missing:
        print("All sidecars up to date.")
        return

    print(f"Computing embeddings for {len(sidecar_missing)} files...")
    for i in range(0, len(sidecar_missing), BATCH_SIZE):
        batch = sidecar_missing[i:i + BATCH_SIZE]
        texts = [p.read_text() for p in batch]
        embeddings = embed_texts(texts)
        for path, embedding in zip(batch, embeddings):
            sidecar = path.with_suffix(".embed.json")
            sidecar.write_text(json.dumps(embedding))
            print(f"  Written: {sidecar.name}")

    print(f"Done. Commit the .embed.json files in {CORPUS_DIR}.")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Preventing Flaky E2E Tests from LLM Variance

E2E tests that assert on specific response text will fail intermittently as the LLM output varies across runs. Three disciplines keep E2E tests reliably green without abandoning meaningful assertions:

# tests/e2e/helpers.py — helpers for non-determinism-safe assertions

def assert_ai_response_quality(response_text: str, topic_keywords: list[str]) -> None:
    """
    Assert that an AI response is plausibly on-topic without checking exact wording.
    Fails only if the response is empty, very short, or contains an error marker.
    """
    assert response_text, "Response must not be empty"
    assert len(response_text) > 30, f"Response suspiciously short: {len(response_text)} chars"
    assert "error" not in response_text.lower(), "Response contains error text"
    assert "undefined" not in response_text.lower(), "Response contains undefined"

    # At least one topic keyword should appear (case-insensitive)
    lowered = response_text.lower()
    matched = [kw for kw in topic_keywords if kw.lower() in lowered]
    assert matched, (
        f"No topic keywords found in response. "
        f"Expected one of: {topic_keywords}. "
        f"Got: {response_text[:200]}"
    )

# Usage in a test:
# assert_ai_response_quality(response_text, topic_keywords=["retrieval", "RAG", "context"])


# Retry helper for E2E LLM calls that occasionally time out
import time
from playwright.sync_api import Page, TimeoutError as PWTimeout

def wait_for_ai_response(page: Page, selector: str, timeout_ms: int = 30_000) -> str:
    """Wait for AI response element to contain non-trivial text."""
    deadline = time.monotonic() + timeout_ms / 1000
    while time.monotonic() < deadline:
        try:
            el = page.locator(selector)
            el.wait_for(state="visible", timeout=5_000)
            text = el.inner_text().strip()
            if len(text) > 20:
                return text
        except PWTimeout:
            pass
        time.sleep(1.0)
    raise TimeoutError(f"AI response in '{selector}' never received meaningful text within {timeout_ms}ms")


# Parametrize E2E tests to run with multiple queries and accept any passing:
# If one query triggers an unusual LLM response, the others still cover the feature.
import pytest

@pytest.mark.parametrize("query,keywords", [
    ("What is RAG?",      ["retrieval", "augmented", "generation"]),
    ("Explain embeddings",["vector", "embedding", "representation"]),
])
def test_summarise_on_topic(authenticated_page, query, keywords):
    page = authenticated_page
    page.goto(f"{__import__('os').environ['E2E_BASE_URL']}/summarise")
    page.fill("[data-testid='query-input']", query)
    page.click("[data-testid='submit-btn']")
    response = wait_for_ai_response(page, "[data-testid='ai-response']")
    assert_ai_response_quality(response, keywords)
Cost and Latency Budgeting Across Test Tiers

Integration and E2E tests consume real LLM tokens. Left unchecked, a test suite that runs 50 integration tests on every merge can cost hundreds of dollars per week. Define per-run token budgets and fail the suite if a single test exceeds its budget — this surfaces tests that accidentally send huge prompts to the LLM.

# tests/integration/conftest.py — token budget enforcement

import pytest
from unittest.mock import patch
from agents.llm_gateway import LLMGateway

TOKEN_BUDGET_PER_TEST = 4_000   # tokens in + out per integration test
LATENCY_BUDGET_MS = 10_000      # hard ceiling on per-call latency

class TokenCounter:
    def __init__(self) -> None:
        self.prompt_tokens = 0
        self.completion_tokens = 0

    @property
    def total(self) -> int:
        return self.prompt_tokens + self.completion_tokens

@pytest.fixture(autouse=True)
def enforce_token_budget(request):
    """Wrap every integration test with a token counter.
    Fail the test if it exceeds TOKEN_BUDGET_PER_TEST."""
    counter = TokenCounter()

    original_complete = LLMGateway.complete

    def tracked_complete(self, prompt, **kwargs):
        result = original_complete(self, prompt, **kwargs)
        # Access usage metadata from the last API response (stored on gateway)
        usage = getattr(self, "_last_usage", None)
        if usage:
            counter.prompt_tokens += usage.get("prompt_tokens", 0)
            counter.completion_tokens += usage.get("completion_tokens", 0)
        return result

    with patch.object(LLMGateway, "complete", tracked_complete):
        yield counter

    if counter.total > TOKEN_BUDGET_PER_TEST:
        pytest.fail(
            f"Token budget exceeded in {request.node.name}: "
            f"{counter.total} tokens used (budget: {TOKEN_BUDGET_PER_TEST}). "
            f"Reduce prompt size or split the test."
        )


# CI cost report: summarise tokens used across the integration suite
# scripts/report_test_costs.py
import json, sys
COST_PER_1K_TOKENS = 0.002  # example: gpt-4o-mini input

def report(junit_xml_path: str) -> None:
    import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET
    tree = ET.parse(junit_xml_path)
    root = tree.getroot()
    total_tokens = sum(
        int(tc.find(".//property[@name='tokens_used']").get("value", 0))
        for tc in root.iter("testcase")
        if tc.find(".//property[@name='tokens_used']") is not None
    )
    cost = (total_tokens / 1000) * COST_PER_1K_TOKENS
    print(f"Integration test run: {total_tokens:,} tokens — estimated cost: ${cost:.4f}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    report(sys.argv[1])
Summary

Integration tests and E2E tests are both necessary, but they own different boundaries. Integration tests verify that one AI pipeline's components compose correctly — they use a real LLM with a controlled corpus and assert on schema, latency, and structural properties. E2E tests verify that the full user-facing journey works — they use Playwright, assert on UI state, and accept output variance by testing
presence
rather than
content equality
Keep integration tests in CI on merge to main (gated by
INTEGRATION_TEST_ENV=true

), and E2E tests post-deploy to staging. Avoid running either on every push — the cost and latency of real LLM calls makes them unsuitable as the primary fast-feedback loop. Unit and snapshot tests own that role.
